title: Game Theory in Social Work: Coalitions and Equilibria
description: Learn how game theory principles—coalitions, Nash equilibrium, and strategic incentives—apply to real social work scenarios involving resource allocation, agency coordination, and client advocacy.

nodes.title: The Shelter Coalition Dilemma
nodes.content: You're a social work coordinator for a mid-sized city's homeless services. Five shelter agencies operate separately, each serving 40-60 people nightly. The city allocates $500K to improve services, but the Request for Proposals requires submitting a unified plan. The agencies face a classic game theory problem: should they cooperate (pool resources, reduce duplication, serve clients better collectively) or each pursue individual interests (maintain autonomy, compete for funding, risk inefficiency)? This scenario will teach you how game theory—the study of strategic decision-making—helps social workers navigate when individual incentives don't align with collective good.

nodes.title: Choose Your Negotiating Strategy
nodes.content: As the coordinator, you must choose how to approach the five agencies. Will you advocate for a binding cooperative agreement, or will you work within each agency's independent interests?

choices.label: Foster a formal coalition with binding agreements (Cooperative Game Theory)
Navigate each agency's individual interests strategically (Non-Cooperative Game Theory)

nodes.title: Coalition Theory and the Core Solution
nodes.content: In cooperative game theory, players can form binding agreements. Your goal is to find allocations in the 'core'—arrangements where no subset of agencies would benefit from breaking away. For your shelter coalition, this means: (1) Identify each agency's unique contribution (intake capacity, counseling expertise, housing stock, medical care). (2) Design a resource distribution where each agency gets credit proportional to what it brings AND receives benefits exceeding what it could get independently or in smaller coalitions. (3) Create governance ensuring all members have meaningful control—no agency feels exploited. If you do this right, the coalition is self-sustaining; each member would actually be worse off leaving.

nodes.title: Three Shelters Form a Coalition
nodes.content: You facilitate an agreement between Bridges Shelter (strong intake processing), Hope House (specialized youth placement), and Safe Haven (long-term transitional housing). Together they design a unified intake where Bridges handles screening and initial triage, Hope House specializes in youth transitions, and Safe Haven owns long-term placements. Revenue splits 50% by clients served, 30% by staff contributed, 20% by facility value. Each agency gets veto power on major operational changes. Over six months, administrative costs drop 35%, client outcomes improve 40%, and each agency's leader confirms they gain more than they could alone. This is the 'core' in action—a coalition where no member wants to leave because they genuinely benefit.

nodes.title: How Will You Distribute Coalition Revenue?
nodes.content: Now that the agencies have agreed to form a coalition, you must decide how to allocate the $500K grant. This choice will determine whether the coalition remains stable or fractures.

choices.label: Divide equally—each of the three agencies gets $166,667 regardless of contribution
Allocate based on each agency's marginal contribution to the coalition (Shapley values)

nodes.title: Coalition Breakdown: The Perils of Perceived Unfairness
nodes.content: Four months into the coalition, Safe Haven's new executive director scrutinizes the allocation and realizes they're providing specialized long-term housing (expensive, specialized staff) but receiving only 1/3 of the fund like everyone else. They feel exploited. Meanwhile, Bridges realizes they could secure 25% more by bidding independently. Both agencies exit. Bridges and Hope House try continuing, but without Safe Haven's capacity, youth age out of placement without stable housing. The coalition wasn't in the 'core'—members didn't all perceive the allocation as fair. Game theory lesson: equal distribution sounds fair but ignores differences in contribution. Next time, use Shapley values—calculate each agency's marginal value (what they add beyond others)—to ensure fairness is both real and perceived. Coalitions fail when members feel exploited, even if objectively they gained.

nodes.title: Coalition Thrives: The Power of Fair Allocation
nodes.content: Using Shapley values, you allocate: Bridges $140K (strong intake contribution), Hope House $130K (specialized youth capacity), Safe Haven $230K (long-term housing is bottleneck-resource; losing it breaks the coalition). After one year, the coalition shows: 60% reduction in client handoff time, $180K saved in duplicated admin, 40% improvement in placement success. Most importantly, all leaders report they gain more in coalition than they could alone. The coalition is stable in the 'core.' Two other agencies request to join. You've learned that game theory isn't about being clever—it's about recognizing that stable cooperation requires both actual fairness (real benefit to all) and perceived fairness (members believe they're valued appropriately). When both align, coalitions endure.

nodes.title: Nash Equilibrium and Individual Agency Strategy
nodes.content: In non-cooperative game theory, agencies pursue independent interests without binding agreements. Each reasons: 'Given what others will likely do, what's my best move?' This leads to Nash Equilibrium—no agency can improve by unilaterally changing strategy. For your shelter coordination, you explore: (1) Dominant strategies (best moves regardless of what others do), (2) The Prisoner's Dilemma (all agencies benefit from transparency and coordination, yet each is tempted to withhold information for competitive advantage, leading collectively to worse outcomes), and (3) Mixed strategies (balancing competition with selective cooperation). Your role: reshape incentives and information to nudge agencies toward better equilibria without requiring formal coalition.

nodes.title: Five Agencies Navigate Competing Interests
nodes.content: Safe Harbor, Community Care, Hope Center, Bridge Services, and New Beginnings bid independently for $500K. Game theory reveals a Prisoner's Dilemma: each agency spends 60+ hours preparing proposals (fearing aggressive competition), the city receives five overlapping bids, and funds only 3 at lower amounts than coordinated proposals would secure. If all five had agreed to specialize and co-submit one integrated proposal, they'd save 200+ hours and likely secure more funding. Yet individually, each fears unilateral cooperation while others bid hard. This bad Nash Equilibrium is individually rational but collectively inefficient. Your strategy: reshape incentives by proposing the city offer 15% funding bonuses for co-submitted proposals. Suddenly mutual cooperation becomes each agency's best individual response.

nodes.title: Navigating Non-Cooperative Settings with Game Theory Insight
nodes.content: Rather than forcing a formal coalition, you use game theory to improve outcomes. You share anonymized data showing all agencies that past aggressive bidding yielded low success rates, propose city funding bonuses for joint proposals, and facilitate specialization discussions. The five agencies submit three integrated proposals. Result: 25% more total funding secured, in less combined staff time. No formal coalition needed. You've learned that real social work often operates in non-cooperative settings where formal agreements fail. But understanding what drives behavior (game theory incentives) lets you reshape systems so agencies choose cooperation as their best individual response. It's messier than elegant coalitions, but often more realistic and durable.
